sections.heading: Definition and Sites
sections.content: Bladder cancer is a malignancy arising from the urothelium that lines the urinary tract from the renal pelvis to the proximal two-thirds of the urethra. The bladder is the most common site, but urothelial carcinoma can also develop in the renal pelvis, ureters, and urethra.

sections.heading: Classification and Categories
sections.content: The 2022 World Health Organization (WHO) classification of tumors of the urinary system is used for grading and subtyping. The clinical spectrum is divided into three categories based on prognosis and management: non–muscle-invasive bladder cancer (NMIBC), muscle-invasive bladder cancer (MIBC), and metastatic disease. NMIBC includes tumors confined to the urothelium (Ta), carcinoma in situ (CIS), and tumors invading the lamina propria (T1). MIBC is defined by invasion into the muscularis propria (≥T2) and includes stages II through IV A. Metastatic disease (stage IVB) involves distant lymph nodes or visceral metastases. The depth of invasion is the most important determinant of prognosis and treatment.

sections.heading: Histologic Subtypes
sections.content: Although urothelial carcinoma is the predominant histology, bladder cancer encompasses a diverse group of histologic subtypes, including squamous cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, small cell carcinoma, and other rare variants, which may have different biological behaviors and treatment implications.

sections.heading: Diagnosis and Staging
sections.content: The diagnosis is confirmed by transurethral resection of bladder tumor (TURBT) with pathological examination, and staging is determined using the American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) TNM system, 8th edition. Risk stratification for NMIBC is based on the American Urological Association (AUA)/Society of Urologic Oncology (SUO) guidelines, incorporating factors such as tumor grade, size, multiplicity, and presence of CIS.
